items.title: Spam filter (narrow AI, classical ML)
items.scenario: An email provider trains a classifier on millions of labeled messages (spam vs not spam). At inference, each new email is scored in milliseconds; users never label training data themselves.
items.explanation: This is narrow AI: one task, fixed objective, learned from data. It sits under the AI umbrella via machine learning—not general intelligence.

items.title: Hospital sepsis alert (narrow AI, high stakes)
items.scenario: A clinical decision-support model ingests vitals and lab trends, then flags patients at elevated sepsis risk. Clinicians review alerts; the model does not autonomously prescribe treatment.
items.explanation: AI here automates a prediction within a human workflow. Responsible deployment (oversight, bias checks) matters—see Responsible & Ethical GenAI for governance framing at product scale.

items.title: Chess engine without learning (still AI)
items.scenario: A chess program evaluates millions of board positions per second using search and hand-crafted evaluation rules. It never trains on historical games yet beats most humans.
items.explanation: Under the broad AI definition, search and planning count as AI even without ML. Colloquial 'AI = ML' omits this branch.

items.title: Voice assistant routing (hybrid AI)
items.scenario: A smart speaker uses wake-word detection (learned model), intent classification (learned), and a scripted fallback tree for unsupported commands ('I can't do that yet').
items.explanation: Production systems often combine learned components and explicit rules—illustrating that AI products are engineered pipelines, not a single algorithm.
